mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Algeria on?
mapQuestions.answer: Africa, on the north coast facing the Mediterranean. Algeria is the LARGEST country in Africa and the 10th largest in the world 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Algeria?
mapQuestions.answer: Algiers, on the Mediterranean coast. Its old town (the "Casbah") is a UNESCO World Heritage site, with whitewashed houses tumbling down a hillside to the sea.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Algeria speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Arabic and Tamazight (the Berber language) are both official. French is widely spoken too, because of the long colonial era that ended in 1962.

mapQuestions.question: How much of Algeria is desert?
mapQuestions.answer: The Sahara covers about 80% of Algeria. The vast majority of Algerians live in the small strip along the Mediterranean coast in the north.

mapQuestions.question: When did Algeria become independent?
mapQuestions.answer: In 1962, after a long and difficult war of independence against France that had ruled Algeria for 132 years. Independence Day is celebrated on 5 July every year.

foods.name: Couscous
foods.description: Tiny steamed semolina grains served with stewed lamb, chickpeas and seven vegetables (turnip, carrot, courgette, pumpkin, etc.). The national Friday dish, eaten with the whole family.

foods.name: Chorba frik
foods.description: A spiced lamb-and-cracked-wheat soup with tomato, coriander and chickpeas. Eaten to break the fast during Ramadan, in every home in the country.

foods.name: Mhajeb
foods.description: A folded, layered semolina flatbread filled with a tomato, onion and pepper relish. Pan-fried until crisp, eaten with mint tea. The Algerian street snack.

foods.name: Makroud
foods.description: Diamond-shaped semolina pastries stuffed with date paste, deep-fried and dipped in honey or syrup. Served at celebrations and with strong sweet coffee.

foods.name: Shakshuka
foods.description: Eggs poached in a bubbling tomato-and-pepper sauce, eaten with bread for breakfast or supper. Popular all across North Africa.

foods.name: Lamb tajine
foods.description: A slow-cooked lamb stew with prunes, almonds, cinnamon and honey, cooked in a clay tajine pot. Sweet and savoury together.

scavengerItems.label: The Sahara dunes (Algeria has some of the biggest sand seas on Earth)
A Berber (Amazigh) tent in a desert oasis
The white-and-blue Casbah of Algiers tumbling down to the sea
A man in a "burnous" (a long woollen hooded cloak)
A camel caravan crossing the dunes
A 2,000-year-old Roman ruin (Algeria has some of the best preserved on Earth)
A "Les Fennecs" (Algerian football team) shirt, named after the desert fox
A real fennec fox (Algeria's national animal, tiny ears the size of its head)
Date palms in an oasis (Algeria is one of the world's top date producers)
The Tassili n'Ajjer prehistoric rock paintings (8,000+ years old)
The Roman ruins at Tipaza, perched on the Mediterranean cliff
A Tuareg "Blue Man" in indigo robes that have stained his skin slightly blue

geoMatches.description: The capital and biggest city, on the Mediterranean coast. The old "Casbah" is a maze of stairs and tiny streets running up a steep hillside.
The vast desert covers 80% of the country. Huge dune fields ("ergs"), oasis towns, and ancient caravan routes.
The "city of bridges". The old city sits ON a high plateau, with a deep gorge running through the middle. Seven dramatic bridges span it.
A Roman coastal city with mosaic-floored villas and a theatre looking out at the Mediterranean. The novelist Albert Camus loved this spot.
A vast sandstone plateau in the southern Sahara, covered in prehistoric rock paintings showing giraffes, hippos and elephants from when the Sahara was green grassland (8,000+ years ago).
The second city, on the Mediterranean coast. The birthplace of "raï" music, with a long mix of Arab, Berber, Spanish and French influences.

senses.smell: Cardamom and rose water in pastries, mint tea steaming, freshly baked semolina bread, hot Sahara sand, sea salt and orange blossom in coastal Algiers…
senses.hear: Arabic radio playing in cafés, the call to prayer drifting across cities at sunset, "Azul!" in Berber villages, the wind shifting Sahara dunes, raï music with strong percussion and electric guitar…
senses.taste: Sweet honey-soaked makroud pastries, fluffy couscous, smoky lamb tajine, syrupy mint tea poured from high in the air, fresh dates straight from a palm tree…
senses.feel: Hot Sahara sun in the day and cool desert night air, fine yellow sand running through your fingers, soft wool of a Berber rug, smooth Roman mosaics under bare feet, Mediterranean spray on Algiers harbour wall…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How big is the Sahara, really?
stories.body: The Sahara is the biggest hot desert on Earth, about 9 million square kilometres, roughly the same size as the entire continental United States. Algeria contains the second-largest share of the Sahara (after Mali). The desert was not always desert though. About 8,000 years ago, the Sahara was a lush green savanna full of giraffes, elephants and lions. We know this because of the incredible Tassili n'Ajjer rock paintings in southern Algeria, drawn by people who lived there when it was green. Then the climate shifted and the Sahara dried out. The descendants of those green-Sahara peoples are believed to be today's Berber and Tuareg desert peoples.

stories.question: Why is there so much Ancient Rome in Algeria?
stories.body: For about 600 years (from the 1st century BC to the 5th century AD), the entire Algerian coast was part of the Roman Empire, called the province of "Mauretania Caesariensis". The Romans built whole cities here: Timgad, Djemila, Tipaza, Cherchell. They are still standing today, with paved streets, theatres, public baths and triumphal arches. Timgad in particular is one of the best-preserved Roman cities anywhere on Earth, sometimes called "the African Pompeii". And the Roman writer Saint Augustine, one of the most influential thinkers in Western philosophy, was born in Roman Algeria in the year 354.

stories.question: Who are the Tuareg?
stories.body: The Tuareg are a Berber-speaking nomadic people who have crossed the Sahara on camels for thousands of years. They live across southern Algeria, Mali, Niger, Libya and Burkina Faso. Tuareg men wear long indigo robes and wrap their faces in a long indigo turban (the "tagelmust"), partly for protection from sand and partly as a social custom. The deep blue indigo dye rubs off slightly onto their skin, which is why Europeans sometimes called them "the Blue People". Tuareg society is matrilineal in some ways (children belong to the mother's family, not the father's), and they have their own alphabet (Tifinagh) that goes back over 2,000 years.

stories.question: Why was Algerian independence so hard-fought?
stories.body: France took over Algeria in 1830 and ruled it for 132 years, longer than it ruled almost anywhere else. Unlike most French colonies, Algeria was treated as part of France itself, with hundreds of thousands of French settlers (called "pieds-noirs") moving in over generations. When the Algerian independence movement started its war in 1954, it became one of the longest, hardest independence struggles in modern African history. Independence was finally won in July 1962. Today 5 July is Algeria's Independence Day, and Algerians remember the struggle deeply. The history is sensitive and painful on both sides, and still shapes Algerian and French relations today.
stories.thinkingQuestion: It took 132 years and a long struggle for Algeria to become independent. What does that teach us about how big changes happen in history?

stories.question: What is raï music?
stories.body: "Raï" (pronounced "rye") means "opinion" in Algerian Arabic. It started in the 1920s and 30s in the port city of Oran, sung by women in bars by the docks, mixing Berber rhythms, Andalusian Arab songs, French chansons, and the new sound of jazz. By the 1980s singers like Cheb Khaled (born in Oran) had turned raï into a worldwide pop sound with electric keyboards and synths, sometimes called "the Algerian blues". Cheb Khaled's song "Aïcha" was one of the biggest French-language hits of the 1990s. Today raï is one of Algeria's biggest cultural exports, and Oran is still its capital.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Algeria so far?
Have you tried saying "shukran" or "azul" to anyone? How did they respond?
Coast, mountains, Sahara, city, which has felt the most "Algeria"?
How does the Sahara feel different from any other landscape you have been in?
If we could see ONE Roman ruin in Algeria, which would you pick?
Algeria has French, Arabic, Berber AND its own North African identity. How does that feel as you move around?

wordSearch.hint: Algerian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Fennec fox
animals.description: Algeria's national animal. Tiny desert fox with enormous bat-like ears, found in the Sahara.

animals.name: Dromedary camel
animals.description: The single-hump desert camel. Tuareg families still use them as transport and as a source of milk and meat.

animals.name: Berber goat
animals.description: In every Algerian mountain village, kept for milk and meat.

animals.name: Berber horse
animals.description: An ancient North African breed, sturdy and small, used by Berber riders for thousands of years.

animals.name: Bonelli's eagle
animals.description: A medium-sized eagle of the Algerian mountains and Saharan cliffs.

animals.name: Sand cat
animals.description: A small wild cat that lives only in deserts. Sandy-coloured, very hard to spot.

animals.name: Barbary macaque
animals.description: The only monkey native to North Africa, in the cedar forests of northern Algeria and Morocco.
